section.title: Abram and Lot Part
section.sectionRationale: Restored to the land after Egypt, Abram faces a new test: strife over land forces him to part from Lot. The unit runs from the return, through the generous parting and Lot's fateful choice, to God's renewal of the promise, and it holds together as a study in faith that lets go and trusts God to provide.
section.thesis: Abram trusts the promise enough to give Lot first choice of the land, and though Lot grasps the best for himself, God confirms to open-handed Abram the whole land and a countless offspring.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the return from Egypt and the strife between herdsmen; Abram's generous offer and Lot's choice toward Sodom; and the LORD's renewal of the land and offspring promise.

units.label: Back in the land, and strife

verses.text: So Abram went up out of Egypt into the Negev, he and his wife and all his possessions, and Lot was with him.
And Abram had become extremely wealthy in livestock and silver and gold.
From the Negev he journeyed from place to place toward Bethel, until he came to the place between Bethel and Ai where his tent had formerly been pitched,
to the site where he had built the altar. And there Abram called on the name of the LORD.
Now Lot, who was traveling with Abram, also had flocks and herds and tents.
But the land was unable to support both of them while they stayed together, for they had so many possessions that they were unable to coexist.
And there was discord between the herdsmen of Abram and the herdsmen of Lot. At that time the Canaanites and the Perizzites were also living in the land.
So Abram said to Lot, “Please let there be no {{riv|contention}} between you and me, or between your herdsmen and my herdsmen. After all, we are kinsmen.
Is not the whole land before you? Now separate yourself from me. If you go to the left, I will go to the right; if you go to the right, I will go to the left.”

And Lot looked out and saw that the whole plain of the Jordan, all the way to Zoar, was well watered like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt. (This was before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah.)
So Lot {{bachar|chose}} the whole plain of the Jordan for himself and set out toward the east. And Abram and Lot parted company.
Abram lived in the land of Canaan, but Lot settled in the cities of the plain and pitched his tent toward Sodom.
But the men of Sodom were wicked, sinning greatly against the LORD.
After Lot had departed, the LORD said to Abram, “Now lift up your eyes from the place where you are, and look to the north and south and east and west,
for all the land that you see, I will give to you and your offspring forever.
I will make your {{zera|offspring}} like the dust of the earth, so that if one could count the dust of the earth, then your offspring could be counted.
Get up and walk around the land, through its length and breadth, for I will give it to you.”
So Abram moved his tent and went to live near the Oaks of Mamre at Hebron, where he built an altar to the LORD.

riv.gloss: Strife, quarrel, or contention. The same root names the place Meribah, where Israel later quarrels with God. Here Abram acts to prevent strife within the family, choosing peace over his own rights as the elder and the one to whom the land was promised.

bachar.gloss: To choose or select. Lot chooses for himself the well watered plain, walking by sight toward Sodom. His self-serving choice contrasts sharply with Abram's faith, and it will lead him step by step into the orbit of a doomed and wicked city.

zera.gloss: Seed or offspring. God promises Abram a seed as countless as the dust of the earth, echoing the promise of Genesis 12 and reaching forward to Christ and to all who are Abram's children by faith, Galatians 3:29.

basic.context: Abram comes back from Egypt very rich, and so is his nephew Lot. Their flocks are so large that the land cannot hold them both, and their herdsmen start quarrelling. To keep peace in the family, Abram offers Lot first pick of the land.
basic.hermeneutics: The key question is why Abram, the elder with the promise, gives up his right to choose first. The answer is faith: Abram trusts God to provide, so he can afford to be generous. Lot, by contrast, chooses by what looks best and drifts toward wicked Sodom.
basic.originalLanguages: Abram acts to stop the strife, a word linked to Israel's later quarrelling with God. Lot chooses the good-looking plain for himself, walking by sight. And God promises Abram an offspring, a seed, as countless as the dust, the same word that runs all the way to Christ.
basic.typology: Abram's giving up his rights for the sake of peace points to the way of Christ, who did not grasp but gave. The countless offspring promised here is fulfilled in Jesus and the great family of faith. And Lot pitching his tent toward Sodom warns of the pull of the world.
basic.christ: Abram lets go of the best land and trusts God, and God gives him everything; this is the pattern Jesus lived and taught, that the one who loses his life will find it. The promised seed as numberless as the dust is finally gathered in Christ, in whom people from every nation become Abram's children.
basic.god: God rewards faith with reassurance. The moment Lot leaves, God speaks, confirming to open-handed Abram the whole land and a countless family. He is generous to those who trust Him and lets go, and He is the God who keeps renewing His promises.
basic.crossReferences: Jesus teaches that the meek inherit the earth and that losing your life for His sake is the way to save it. Paul says all who belong to Christ are Abram's offspring. And Hebrews notes Abram lived as a pilgrim, waiting for a better country.
basic.oneStory: Abram trusts the promise enough to hold his rights loosely and let Lot grab the best. God answers by pledging him the whole land and a countless seed. This is the way of faith that runs through the Bible to Jesus, who gave up everything and so inherited all things, and who makes a countless family from every nation.

context.literary: This chapter deliberately contrasts two men and two ways of seeing. Lot looks and chooses by sight; Abram is told by God to look and is given by grace. The narrator drops ominous notes, toward Sodom and the men of Sodom were wicked, that set up chapters 14, 18, and 19.
context.historical: The plain of the Jordan around Sodom was lush and well watered before its destruction, a natural prize for a herdsman. Canaanites and Perizzites still filled the land, so Abram's confidence rests on promise, not present possession.
context.canonical: The renewed promise of land and countless offspring builds on Genesis 12 and anticipates the fuller covenant of Genesis 15 and 17. Lot's drift toward Sodom sets up his rescue in chapter 14 and the judgment of Sodom in chapter 19, and the New Testament makes him a warning and Abram a model of faith.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To contrast the faith of Abram, who trusts God enough to yield his rights, with the sight-driven grasping of Lot, and to show God rewarding faith by renewing the promise of land and offspring.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the two men's choices and God's response. Abram's peace-making generosity is held up as commendable, while Lot's self-seeking drift is presented as a warning, though neither is a rigid rule for dividing property.

debates.question: Was Lot's choice merely unwise, or morally culpable?

views.label: Understandable but spiritually reckless
views.case: Lot chose by what looked best and edged toward Sodom; the text stresses his walking by sight and the wickedness of the city, framing his choice as a drift into danger.

views.label: A neutral practical decision
views.case: Some read Lot as simply taking a reasonable offer of good grazing land, with the tragedy lying in later developments rather than in the choice itself.

debates.bearing: Either way the contrast with Abram stands: faith holds possessions loosely and trusts God, while choosing by sight alone can carry us, step by step, toward what will harm us.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on a play of seeing and choosing. Abram moves to end the riv, strife, choosing peace over his rightful first claim. Lot then bachar, chooses, the well watered plain for himself, guided by his eyes toward Sodom, the mirror opposite of Abram's faith. And when Lot is gone, God tells Abram to lift up his own eyes, not to grab but to receive, promising him a zera, offspring, like the dust of the earth. The same seed promise that began in Genesis 12 is enlarged here and will run through Scripture to Christ and to all who are his by faith.

typology.type: Abram yielding his rights for peace
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of Christ, who did not grasp equality with God but emptied Himself, Philippians 2, and who taught that losing one's life is the way to find it.

typology.type: Offspring like the dust of the earth
typology.fulfillment: The countless family of faith gathered in Christ, in whom Abram becomes father of many nations, Galatians 3:29.

typology.type: Lot pitching his tent toward Sodom
typology.fulfillment: A standing warning against the pull of the world, later used by Jesus, remember Lot's wife, Luke 17:32.

study.christ: Abram embodies the way of Christ before Christ: he lets go of the best land, trusting God rather than grasping, and God gives him everything. Jesus lived this to the full, refusing to grasp, emptying Himself, and so inheriting all things and being highly exalted. The promise of a seed as countless as the dust finds its yes in Him, for in Christ people from every nation become Abram's offspring and heirs of the promise. Where Lot walks by sight toward a doomed city, Christ calls His people to walk by faith toward the city that is to come.
study.god: God honours faith. No sooner has Abram surrendered his choice than the LORD speaks, expanding the promise: all the land in every direction, and offspring beyond counting. He is generous precisely to the open-handed, reassuring the man who trusted Him rather than clutching at security. He renews His word freely, showing Himself a covenant God who delights to give, and who is not bound by present appearances, for the land is still full of Canaanites when He pledges it to Abram forever.

crossReferences.note: Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth, the way of Abram over against grasping Lot.
Christ did not grasp but emptied Himself and was exalted, the pattern Abram foreshadows.
If you belong to Christ, you are Abraham's seed and heirs according to the promise.
Jesus recalls Lot and Sodom as a warning; remember Lot's wife.

study.oneStory: Given the choice, Abram trusts the promise enough to let Lot seize the best, and God responds by confirming to him the whole land and a countless seed. This is the way of faith that threads through the Bible: those who lose their lives find them, those who hold loosely receive most. It runs on to Jesus, who grasped nothing and inherited everything, and who gathers Abram's numberless family from every nation.

quiz.q: Why did Abram let Lot choose first?

options.text: He trusted God to provide, so he could hold his rights loosely and seek peace.
options.note: Abram's generosity flows from faith in God's promise.

options.text: He was afraid of Lot and gave in to avoid a fight.
options.note: It was faith and peace-making, not fear, that moved Abram.

options.text: God had commanded him to give Lot the best land.
options.note: God gave no such command; Abram chose to be generous.

options.text: As the elder, he wanted to keep peace in the family.
options.note: True, and the deeper root was his faith that God would still provide.

quiz.q: What guided Lot's choice of the Jordan plain?

options.text: What looked best to the eye, drawing him toward Sodom.
options.note: Lot walked by sight, and the text notes Sodom's wickedness.

options.text: A word from God directing him there.
options.note: God spoke to Abram, not to Lot, about the land.

options.text: A desire to protect Abram from danger.
options.note: Lot chose for himself, not out of concern for Abram.

options.text: The good grazing the well watered land offered.
options.note: True on the surface, but the deeper issue was choosing by sight toward a wicked city.

quiz.q: What did God do right after Lot departed?

options.text: He confirmed to Abram the whole land and a countless offspring.
options.note: God rewarded Abram's faith by renewing the promise.

options.text: He rebuked Abram for giving up the best land.
options.note: God did not rebuke him; He reassured and blessed him.

options.text: He immediately destroyed Sodom.
options.note: Sodom's judgment comes later, in chapter 19.

options.text: He told Abram to look in every direction at the land He would give.
options.note: True; part of the renewed promise, which also included countless offspring.

quiz.q: How does Abram's example point to Jesus?

options.text: He held his rights loosely and trusted God, the way Christ did not grasp but gave.
options.note: Abram foreshadows the self-giving way of Christ.

options.text: He seized the best for himself as Jesus did.
options.note: Neither grasped; both gave, which is the point.

options.text: He built a great city like the one Jesus builds.
options.note: Abram built altars and lived as a pilgrim; the link is faith, not city-building.

options.text: He chose peace over conflict, as Christ makes peace.
options.note: True and good, though the sharpest link is holding possessions loosely in trust.

quiz.q: What is the promise of offspring like the dust of the earth about?

options.text: A countless family, fulfilled in Christ and all who are Abram's by faith.
options.note: The seed promise runs to Christ and the worldwide family of faith.

options.text: Only the physical nation of Israel and no one else.
options.note: Israel is included, but the New Testament widens it to all who believe.

options.text: A promise that quietly failed since Abram was still childless.
options.note: The promise did not fail; God fulfilled it in time and ultimately in Christ.

options.text: A reference to Abram's growing wealth in livestock.
options.note: It speaks of descendants, not livestock or riches.

questions: Abram could be generous because he trusted God to provide. Where would trusting God free you to hold your possessions or rights more loosely?
Lot chose by what looked best and drifted toward trouble. Where are you tempted to make big decisions by appearances rather than by faith?
Abram sought peace with a relative rather than winning the argument. Is there a family tension where you could choose peace over your rights?
God reassured Abram right after he let go. When have you found that surrendering something to God was met by His provision?
Jesus said the one who loses his life will find it. What is one thing you sense God asking you to release in order to follow Him more freely?

translationNotes.note: The plain is described as like the garden of the LORD and like Egypt, echoing Eden and Abram's recent detour; the parenthesis before the LORD destroyed Sodom warns the reader that this beauty is doomed.
The Oaks, or Terebinths, of Mamre near Hebron become a settled base for Abram; the note that he built an altar marks worship as the anchor of his life in the land.
